title: Throwing a stoneware casserole dish
subtitle: A wide-bellied grogged stoneware dish with a thrown lid, safe for oven-to-table use
excerpt: A grogged stoneware casserole approximately 200 mm in diameter and 100 mm deep, with a separately thrown lid fitted to a flange on the rim. Grogged clay resists thermal shock from direct oven heat better than smooth stoneware. Two small lug handles are added at the leather-hard stage. The dish fires to cone 6 and is rated for direct oven use once properly glaze-fired.

glossaryTerms.definition: the drying stage where clay holds its shape but is still damp enough to trim, carve, and join
fully air-dried but unfired clay: fragile and at maximum absorbency
unfired clay at any drying stage, from wet through to bone-dry
clay that has been through the first firing (cone 06, approximately 1000 degrees C) and is now hard but unglazed and porous
the trimmed-back base ridge a pot sits on
a pyrometric measure of heat-work: cone 06 is approximately 1000 degrees C, cone 6 is approximately 1220 degrees C

content.text: Silica dust is the chronic studio hazard. Silicosis is irreversible. Mix wet whenever possible. Wear a P100 respirator any time raw glaze powders are open. Wet-clean only: no sweeping, no compressed air, no dry sanding indoors.
Ventilation matters. Either a downdraft vent on the kiln or an open window with an extractor pulling air past the kiln. Never open a hot kiln below approximately 80 degrees C.

This casserole is thrown in grogged stoneware: a clay body with coarse particles of fired clay fired into it. The grog opens the structure, giving the finished piece a better coefficient of thermal expansion and making it far more tolerant of rapid temperature changes than smooth stoneware alone. The result is a dish that can go from a cold oven to full heat, or from the oven directly to a trivet on the table, without cracking through thermal shock. The body is approximately 200 mm in diameter and 100 mm deep. A separately thrown lid seats onto a flange left on the rim. Two coil lug handles are attached at the
leather-hard
stage. The piece fires to
cone
6 (approximately 1220 degrees C).
Preparation

Wedge approximately 1,200 g of grogged stoneware for the body, 400 g for the lid, and 100 g for the two handles: a total of around 1,700 g. The grog particles in the clay feel gritty against your hands; this is normal and does not affect throwing, though it is harder on the skin than smooth clay. Throw body and lid in the same session so both dry at the same rate and you can fit them together at leather-hard. Have your calipers ready before you begin throwing, as the lid-gallery measurement is critical.

materials.name: Grogged stoneware
materials.quantity: 1,700 g total (1,200 g body, 400 g lid, 100 g handles)

materials.name: Mid-fire glaze, pre-mixed
materials.quantity: enough to dip body and lid

content.text: Throwing the body

Secure a bat on the wheel head. Centre the 1,200 g of grogged stoneware at medium speed. Grogged clay centres a little more roughly than smooth stoneware; give it an extra pass to confirm it runs true before opening.

Open the centre firmly, pressing down to leave a base approximately 12 mm thick. The casserole base takes direct oven heat; a thinner base risks cracking under thermal stress. Widen the opening to approximately 160 mm using both hands, keeping the floor flat.
Compress the base thoroughly, working from centre to edge in overlapping circles, applying firm downward pressure. This removes any remaining air and aligns the clay particles for strength.

Pull the walls upward in three or four passes, keeping wall thickness at 8 to 10 mm. A casserole does not need to be thin: the extra mass helps the piece hold heat at the table and adds structural strength for oven cycling. Aim for a finished height of approximately 110 mm to allow for a 10 mm trimming allowance.
Allow the rim to flare slightly outward to a diameter of approximately 200 mm. This wide rim will become the gallery onto which the lid seats. Do not collar the rim inward.

Create the lid gallery: use a finger on the inside of the rim and a supporting finger outside to press a flat ledge approximately 8 mm wide and 6 mm below the top of the rim. This ledge is where the lid's flange will rest. Measure the internal diameter of the gallery at this point with calipers and write it down. This measurement dictates the lid diameter.
Smooth the exterior with the wooden rib and the rim with a damp chamois. Remove excess water from the interior. Wire the piece off the bat and leave the bat and piece in place overnight.
Throwing the lid
Centre 400 g of grogged stoneware on a fresh bat. Open and flatten the clay to create a wide shallow dome or flat disc shape, roughly 20 mm deep.

Pull a low flange down and outward from the underside of the lid edge so that it fits inside the gallery of the body. Check the fit against the caliper measurement from step 6 above. The flange should slide into the gallery with approximately 2 mm of clearance on each side to allow for any warping during firing.

Add a knob to the lid centre by pulling up a small cylinder of clay from the centre of the lid and shaping it to a comfortable grip. The knob needs to withstand oven temperatures so keep it solid rather than hollow.
Wire the lid off the bat. Set both pieces aside to dry together at the same rate.
Adding the lug handles

When the body has stiffened to leather-hard, roll two short coils from the remaining 100 g of grogged stoneware, each approximately 60 mm long and 15 mm in diameter. Flatten each coil slightly with your palm to form a lug shape.
Score both the body at the attachment points and the ends of the lug with a needle tool in a crosshatch pattern. Apply a thin coat of slip to both scored surfaces.

Press each lug firmly onto the body at the scored points, positioned opposite each other, below the gallery ledge. Blend the join at both ends with a fingertip, pressing firmly outward to seat the lug cleanly into the body wall. Poorly blended joins crack away during drying.
Cover the assembled casserole and lid together with loose plastic to slow drying evenly across both pieces. Leave for 24 hours before removing the plastic and allowing full drying.
Trimming

Trim the base of the body at leather-hard. Because the lug handles prevent inverting the piece on the wheel head in the usual way, support the piece in a foam ring or a chuck made from a large coil of soft clay. Trim a low wide
foot ring
using the loop tool, leaving the base thickness at a minimum of 10 mm.

Trim the lid on the wheel: invert it and support it centrally. Trim the flange to fit the caliper measurement precisely. The final fit is tested at leather-hard: the lid should drop onto the gallery and sit level with a small amount of play.
Drying and bisque firing
Slow-dry the piece under plastic for the first 2 days, with the lid sitting loosely on the body so both pieces dry at the same rate. Remove plastic and allow to reach the
bone-dry
stage. This is
greenware
: handle it carefully as bone-dry clay is brittle. Bisque fire body and lid together at cone 06 (approximately 1000 degrees C). The
bisque
firing burns off all organic material and prepares the clay body for glazing. Fire body and lid sitting together so they can be checked for fit immediately on cooling.
Glazing
Glaze the interior of the body by pouring glaze in and swirling to coat the base and walls to a height of approximately 80 mm. Tip the excess glaze out carefully and allow to dry.

Dip the exterior of the body up to within 8 mm of the foot ring. Clean any drips from the foot ring and the lower 8 mm of the base with a damp sponge. Leave a generous unglazed margin: the wide base of the casserole means even a small amount of glaze flow can fuse it to the shelf.
Glaze the lid exterior by dipping, leaving the underside of the flange unglazed. Glaze on the flange gallery surface will fuse lid to body during firing. Wipe the flange clean.
Fire the lid propped slightly open or separately on its own shelf to avoid any risk of glaze binding the flange to the gallery during the firing.
Glaze firing

Fire to cone 6 (approximately 1220 degrees C). Apply kiln wash to the shelf under the casserole body as insurance against any glaze run. Cool the kiln naturally: do not force-cool. The piece is rated for direct oven use after the glaze firing, provided the glaze is fully melted and the body is without cracks. Thermal shock resistance comes from the grogged clay body, not from any special oven-safe coating: the integrity of every joint and the absence of cracks in the greenware and bisque stages are what make it safe in the oven.
Care

Always place the casserole into a cold or gently warming oven. Do not transfer it into a pre-heated oven at full temperature. The grogged clay body handles oven cycling well, but sudden extreme temperature shifts can still cause dunting. Avoid placing the hot dish on a cold wet surface. Hand-washing is safest. The piece is likely dishwasher-safe at cone 6, but repeated harsh cycles may eventually affect the glaze surface.
Variations
Oval form: throw a round body. Then, at leather-hard and before adding the lug handles, press gently inward at two opposite points to alter it to an oval.
Two-glaze treatment: dip the exterior in one colour and the interior in a contrasting colour. This gives a classic thrown-stoneware look.
Individual serving dishes: use the same form at 300 g of clay per body. This produces individual casseroles about 120 mm across.

rows.problem: Lug handle cracks off at the join during drying
rows.cause: The lug was not scored and slipped adequately, or it dried faster than the body
rows.fix: Score and slip both surfaces thoroughly; keep the piece under plastic for at least 2 days after attaching handles to equalise drying

rows.problem: Lid does not seat flat in the gallery
rows.cause: Lid flange diameter is slightly too large, or the body gallery warped during drying
rows.fix: At leather-hard, trim the flange incrementally and check fit frequently with calipers; accept 2 mm of total play as normal

rows.problem: Base cracks radially in the kiln
rows.cause: Base too thick in the centre relative to the walls, causing differential shrinkage, or the base was not compressed adequately
rows.fix: Compress the base firmly at the throwing stage; ensure even thickness by checking with a needle tool before pulling walls

rows.problem: Glaze pools heavily at the base exterior and fuses to the kiln shelf
rows.cause: Glaze applied too thickly, or too close to the foot ring
rows.fix: Leave at least 8 mm unglazed above the foot ring; apply kiln wash to the shelf; if glaze still runs, use a shorter dip time
